subject: english
learning_objectives: Define an adverb and state what it modifies
Explain how adverbs are formed from other word classes
Distinguish adjuncts, disjuncts, and conjuncts with examples

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content: An adverb is a word that modifies verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs.

sections.heading: Formation of Adverbs
sections.content:
Many adverbs are formed from adjectives by adding -ly:
  certain→certainly, fortunate→fortunately, careful→carefully, quick→quickly

Some adverbs showing direction end in -wards:
  downwards, forwards, backwards

Some adverbs expressing manner or viewpoint end in -wise:
  clockwise, foodwise, moneywise

Many adverbs have no special ending:
  always, early, fast, quite, often, very, when, hard, late, so

sections.heading: Types of Adverbs
sections.content:
ADJUNCTS – tell us how, when, where, or to what extent the action of the verb is performed.
  He came at 6 o'clock. (when) | She ran fast. (how)

DISJUNCTS – express the attitude or viewpoint of the speaker.
  Luckily, she arrived. | Frankly, we were in the wrong.
  Ola is certainly the best. | Foolishly, he fell.
  Others: honestly, seriously, strangely, undoubtedly, happily, fortunately

CONJUNCTS – perform a connective function between sentences or clauses.
  It was a hard task; nevertheless, we performed well.
  She is hardworking; besides, she is intelligent.
  Others: consequently, meanwhile, otherwise, similarly, then, alternately
